competence_markers.description: Can the child identify when authority is being exercised fairly versus unfairly?
When given temporary authority, do they apply rules equally?
Can they distinguish between personal disappointment and genuine unfairness?
Do they follow the rules they would enforce on others?
Are they developing awareness that authority carries responsibility?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about someone in charge who you think is fair.
narration_prompts.detail: What do they do that makes them fair? How do you know?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you were in charge of something.
narration_prompts.detail: Were you fair? Did you apply the same rules to everyone?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between "I did not get what I wanted" and "the rules are not being applied equally"?
Tell me about a leader from a story who used their power well.
narration_prompts.detail: What did they do? Why was it good?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a leader from a story who used their power badly.
narration_prompts.detail: What did they do? What happened because of it?

cross_connections.description: How authority is established and why people obey — the political dimension of what this packet teaches personally
The character foundation of ethical authority
Governing yourself fairly is the prerequisite for governing others fairly
Authority within teams — how the person in charge uses their power affects everyone
Unfair authority is a common source of conflict

content.why_this_matters:
You are under authority all the time. Parents tell you what to do. Mentors set the rules. Older students have influence over younger ones. This is normal and necessary — children need guidance, and authority exists for a reason.

But authority can be used well or badly. A person in charge who is fair, who explains their rules, who follows the same rules they enforce, and who uses their authority to help — that person makes things better for everyone. A person in charge who is unfair, who has different rules for their favorites, who uses their authority to get what they want at everyone else's expense — that person makes things worse.

You are learning to tell the difference. And when you are the one with authority — even temporary authority over a game, a task, or a group of younger children — you are learning to use it fairly.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Recognizing fair and unfair authority** — identifying when power is used well versus when it is used poorly
2. **Exercising temporary authority fairly** — applying rules equally when given a leadership role
3. **Understanding that authority creates responsibility** — power is for serving the group, not serving yourself
4. **Distinguishing personal disappointment from genuine unfairness** — learning to tell the difference
5. **Observing leadership in stories and in life** — beginning to build a mental library of good and bad leadership
content.core_content:
### Part 1: People in Charge

All around you, there are people in charge of things. Your parents are in charge of your family. Your mentor is in charge of your learning. When you play a game, someone might be in charge of the rules. When a group works on a project, someone is in charge of the plan.

Being in charge means having authority — the ability to make decisions that affect other people. This is a serious thing. When you are in charge, your decisions do not just affect you. They affect everyone in the group.

Watch the people in charge around you. Ask yourself: Are they fair? Do they apply the same rules to everyone? Do they explain why they make the decisions they make? Do they listen when someone disagrees? Do they follow the rules they enforce? Do they use their authority to help the group, or to help themselves?

These questions will help you recognize the difference between authority used well and authority used badly. And that recognition is the beginning of understanding leadership.

### Part 2: When It Is Your Turn

Sometimes you will be in charge of something — a game, a task, a group of younger children. When this happens, you have a choice about what kind of leader you will be.

The fair leader applies the same rules to everyone. If the rule is "everyone takes turns," then everyone takes turns — including your best friend, including the person you do not like very much. If you change the rules to benefit your friends or to punish people you are annoyed with, you are not being fair. And everyone can see it.

The fair leader explains what is happening. "We are going to do it this way because..." is better than "Because I said so." You do not have to have a long discussion about every decision. But when people understand why, they cooperate better.

The fair leader follows the rules they enforce. If you tell everyone to clean up and then you do not clean up, you have told the group that the rules do not really apply to the person in charge. This is one of the fastest ways to lose people's respect and willingness to cooperate.

### Part 3: Power Is for Serving, Not for Getting

Here is something important to understand early: when you have authority over something, the authority exists to serve the group — not to serve you.

If you are in charge of distributing supplies and you give yourself the best ones, you have used your authority to serve yourself. If you are in charge of choosing teams and you put all the best players on your team, you have used your authority to serve yourself. If you are in charge of a game and you change the rules when you are losing, you have used your authority to serve yourself.

In each case, the authority was given to you for a purpose — to distribute supplies fairly, to create balanced teams, to run a fair game. Using it for your own advantage instead is a betrayal of the trust that came with the authority.

This is a lesson that many adults never learn. Look at the news, at history, at the stories of leaders throughout time. How many of them used their power to serve the people they led? How many used it to serve themselves? The difference between the two is the difference between a leader and a tyrant.

### Part 4: "It Is Not Fair" — Two Different Meanings

When children say "it is not fair," they sometimes mean two very different things.

The first meaning: "I did not get what I wanted." You wanted the blue crayon and someone else got it. You wanted to go first and it was not your turn. This is disappointment. It is real, and it is okay to feel it. But it is not unfairness. Rules that apply equally to everyone are fair, even when the outcome is not what you wanted.

The second meaning: "The rules are being applied differently to different people." One child gets to break the rule without consequence. Another child gets punished for the same thing. The person in charge gives their friends special treatment. This is genuine unfairness. This is worth speaking up about.

Learning to tell the difference — between "I am disappointed because I did not get what I wanted" and "the rules are being applied unequally" — is one of the most important things you can learn at your age. It will serve you for the rest of your life.

### Part 5: Stories of Good and Bad Leaders

Throughout this framework, you will encounter stories of leaders — from history, from biography, from fiction. Pay attention to how they use their authority.

George Washington could have been king. After the American Revolution, he had enough popularity and military support to seize permanent power. Instead, he served as president for two terms and then went home. He gave up power voluntarily — one of the most significant acts of ethical leadership in modern history.

In contrast, throughout history there are countless examples of leaders who seized power and used it to enrich themselves, punish their enemies, and silence anyone who disagreed. The stories of Rome, of medieval Europe, of modern dictators — they all show what happens when authority is used to serve the leader instead of the people.

At your age, the stories are simpler — kings and queens, heroes and villains. But the patterns you notice now are the same patterns you will study in depth as you grow. Start watching for them.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Recognizing Fairness and Unfairness
**Developing well:** The child can identify when authority is being exercised fairly or unfairly — in stories, in life, and in their own behavior. They can articulate why something is fair or unfair.

**Developing slowly:** The child equates "fair" with "what I wanted to happen." They cannot distinguish between personal disappointment and genuine inequality of treatment.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you regularly discussing fairness in concrete situations? The concept develops through application, not abstract instruction.

### 2. Fair Exercise of Authority
**Developing well:** When given temporary authority, the child applies rules equally, including to friends. They resist the temptation to use authority for personal advantage. They explain their decisions.

**Developing slowly:** The child uses temporary authority for personal benefit — choosing themselves first, favoring friends, changing rules when losing. They resist being held accountable for unfair authority.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the child experienced both fair and unfair authority in their own life? Children who have only experienced fair authority may not recognize its importance. Children who have experienced unfair authority may have strong motivation to be fair themselves.

### 3. Authority-Responsibility Connection
**Developing well:** The child demonstrates emerging understanding that being in charge means being responsible for the group, not being served by the group.

**Developing slowly:** The child views authority primarily as a privilege — getting to decide, being first, having control — rather than as a responsibility.
